_meta.description: World transition cutscene dialogue. The liminal moments between worlds — standing at the portal, processing what they've learned, deciding to move forward. For cowir-cutscenes to implement as transition cinematics.
_meta.author: cowir-story

w1_to_w2.title: Phase Transition: Medieval → Suburban
w1_to_w2.context: The party has defeated all four W1 Masterites and discovered that Chancellor Mordaine was designed — placed — by something larger. A portal has appeared in Dr. Temporal's workshop showing parking lots and picket fences.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: Mordaine designed our journey. All of it. The cave. The forest. The Masterites. Us.

pre_portal.speaker: mage
pre_portal.text: She designed a challenge. We happened to be the ones inside it.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: That's not better.

pre_portal.speaker: mage
pre_portal.text: It's not worse either. The design is real. The challenges were real. What we built — the way we fight, the way we adapt — that came from us, not from her.

pre_portal.speaker: rogue
pre_portal.text: Does it count if everything we overcame was built to be overcome?

pre_portal.speaker: cleric
pre_portal.text: Does any game count if the pieces were designed to fit together?
pre_portal.note: The word 'game' lands strangely. First time anyone uses it. The Cleric doesn't seem to notice she said it.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: The diagram — the administrative chart — it wasn't administrative at all. It was a map of phases. And the scholar said there were five more like it.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: This isn't a reward.

pre_portal.speaker: mage
pre_portal.text: No.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: It's the next one.

pre_portal.speaker: fighter
pre_portal.text: We're going anyway.
pre_portal.note: The Fighter steps through first. Always does.

during_transition.speaker: narrator
during_transition.text: The medieval world didn't end behind them — it simply stopped being relevant. Like a chapter in a book no one was reading anymore.

post_portal.speaker: narrator
post_portal.text: The transformation happened between the shimmer and the step.

post_portal.speaker: fighter
post_portal.note: Fighter looks down at his Security Guard uniform. Badge says SECURITY.

post_portal.speaker: cleric
post_portal.text: I think we're still supposed to be helping people.

post_portal.speaker: mage
post_portal.text: New world. New context. Same skills, different costume.

post_portal.speaker: rogue
post_portal.text: I know how this works. I don't know how I know. I just do.

post_portal.speaker: bard
post_portal.note: Bard examines trumpet. Plays a note. Car alarm goes off nearby.

extra_line.speaker: mage
extra_line.text: The combat scripts — they came through. Different syntax, same logic. The automation translated.

extra_line.speaker: bard
extra_line.text: We didn't miss anything back there, did we? I keep feeling like we left something unfinished. Phil said something about dreams — metal walls, blinking lights. I wrote it all down.

w2_to_w3.title: Phase Transition: Suburban → Steampunk
w2_to_w3.context: The party has defeated the suburban Masterites and discovered the HOA President was another designed authority. A portal in the community center basement shows brass gears and steam vents.

pre_portal.speaker: mage
pre_portal.text: Two worlds. Two designed authorities. Two sets of Masterites — same archetypes, different costumes. Warden, Arbiter, Tempo, Curator. The pattern is repeating.

pre_portal.speaker: fighter
pre_portal.text: The Warden of Routine was the same as the Warden of the Old Guard. Not the same person. The same... shape.
pre_portal.note: Especially resonant if Fighter has Warden sub-job.

pre_portal.speaker: cleric
pre_portal.text: She didn't know where she was before. Neither did the Old Guard. They're placed. Someone places them.

pre_portal.speaker: rogue
pre_portal.text: Someone places them and they don't know they've been placed. That's the part that bothers me. Not the placing — the not knowing.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: The crossing guard said she'd been at her corner since before she could remember. The delivery driver said the route never ends. The librarian said the catalog was all there was.

pre_portal.speaker: bard
pre_portal.text: I'm starting to hear a theme. And I don't like the song.

pre_portal.speaker: mage
pre_portal.text: The next world has gears. Clockwork. Precision machinery. If the first world was honest duty and the second was sincere bureaucracy, the third is—

pre_portal.speaker: rogue
pre_portal.text: Systems. Visible systems. The kind that show you how they work.

pre_portal.speaker: fighter
pre_portal.text: Let's find out.

during_transition.speaker: narrator
during_transition.text: The suburb dissolved behind them like a dream you can't quite hold onto after waking — the details fading first, then the feeling, then the certainty that it had been real at all.

during_transition.speaker: narrator
during_transition.text: The air changed. It became thicker. Warmer. It tasted of copper and steam and something mechanical — the specific flavor of air that has been breathed by engines.

post_portal.speaker: narrator
post_portal.text: The transformation this time was louder. Metal clanged into place where polyester had been. Gears spun where zippers had closed. The world wasn't subtle about what it was doing.

post_portal.speaker: fighter
post_portal.text: Armor again. But it's not steel — it's brass. And it's ticking.
post_portal.note: Fighter is now Steamknight. Brass clockwork plate.

post_portal.speaker: cleric
post_portal.text: The prayers are different here. Same words but they arrive through pipes. Like the faith has plumbing.

post_portal.speaker: mage
post_portal.text: The formulae — they've become chemical equations. This is alchemy. Not metaphorical alchemy. Actual reaction chemistry with a magical product.

post_portal.speaker: rogue
post_portal.text: Lockpicks still work. Different locks, same principle. Tumblers are gears now, but a tumbler is a tumbler.

post_portal.speaker: bard
post_portal.text: The trumpet became a steam-whistle. I think I can make it sing. Give me a minute.

post_portal.speaker: mage
post_portal.text: Two worlds of costumes. The skills underneath don't change — just the interface. Whoever is designing this understands something about abstraction.

post_portal.speaker: fighter
post_portal.text: Or they understand something about us.

extra_line.speaker: mage
extra_line.text: The autobattle scripts adapted instantly. Punch-card logic now. Same rules, different encoding. It's like the system wants to be automated.

extra_line.speaker: rogue
extra_line.text: We're stronger than we should be. Whatever these worlds are calibrated for, we've outgrown the calibration. Someone's going to notice.

extra_line.speaker: bard
extra_line.text: I kept a complete record. Every conversation from Harmonia and Maple Heights. Sixty-three NPCs. Forty-one side quests. Twelve hidden items. Every single one of them had something to say that mattered. I don't think the designer expected anyone to hear all of it.
